solution-article.title: Can I program this device to re-route 911 calls to another number?
solution-article.description:
Yes!  Below are the steps to program this auto dialer to re-route 911 calls to any other number (NNNNNNNNNN).

***8888 (hear 1 beep)

*01*1*NNNNNNNNNN** (hear 1 beep)

*03*1*911** (hear 1 beep)

*15*911** (hear 1 beep)
*18*1234567890** (hear 1 beep)
hangup

You can also program this auto dialer to re-route calls from number A to number B, as shown below:

***8888 (hear 1 beep)

*01*1*B** (hear 1 beep)

*03*1*A** (hear 1 beep)

*15*A** (hear 1 beep)
*18*1234567890** (hear 1 beep)
hangup

What if you also want to reroute other numbers e.g. 0911, 1911, etc?
Simply add the other numbers to the registers 03 and 15, as follows:

***8888 (hear 1 beep)

*01*1*B** (hear 1 beep)

*03*1*A1*A2*A3*A4*A5 .... *AN** (hear 1 beep)
*10*1**  (enables the dialing of the second number or PIN) 

*15*A1*A2*A3*A4*A5 .... *AN** (hear 1 beep)
*18*1234567890** (hear 1 beep)
hangup


What if you need to re-route your 911 calls to a carrier that requires 2 sets of numbers (e.g. set 1: a toll-free number, set 2: an access number or pin code)?
You can use register 02 to add that second string of numbers.  You can also add a delay between the first set of numbers (toll-free number) and the second set of numbers using register 06 as follows (10=1 second, 20=2 seconds, 30=3 seconds, 40=4 seconds, 50=5 seconds, etc):

***8888 (hear 1 beep)

*01*1*B** (hear 1 beep)

*02*1*2*C**
*03*1*A1*A2*A3*A4*A5 .... *AN** (hear 1 beep)

*06*1*50**
*10*1**
*15*A1*A2*A3*A4*A5 .... *AN** (hear 1 beep)
*17*1*00*3**
*18*1234567890** (hear 1 beep)
hangup

Where
A (also A1, A2, A3 ...AN) = the numbers dialed by the users that you want to re-route
B = the first set of numbers required by your carrier (access number)
C = the second set of numbers required by your carrier (access code)
A,B & C are any string of numbers (0-9), up to 16 digits long, including #..


If you need any more help, please contact us.
solution-article.desc-un-html:
Yes! Below are the steps to program this auto dialer to re-route 911 calls to any other number (NNNNNNNNNN). ***8888 (hear 1 beep) *01*1*NNNNNNNNNN** (hear 1 beep) *03*1*911** (hear 1 beep) *15*911** (hear 1 beep) *18*1234567890** (hear 1 beep) hangup You can also program this auto dialer to re-route calls from number A to number B, as shown below: ***8888 (hear 1 beep) *01*1*B** (hear 1 beep) *03*1*A** (hear 1 beep) *15*A** (hear 1 beep) *18*1234567890** (hear 1 beep) hangup What if you also want to reroute other numbers e.g. 0911, 1911, etc? Simply add the other numbers to the registers 03 and 15, as follows: ***8888 (hear 1 beep) *01*1*B** (hear 1 beep) *03*1*A1*A2*A3*A4*A5 .... *AN** (hear 1 beep) *10*1** (enables the dialing of the second number or PIN) *15*A1*A2*A3*A4*A5 .... *AN** (hear 1 beep) *18*1234567890** (hear 1 beep) hangup What if you need to re-route your 911 calls to a carrier that requires 2 sets of numbers (e.g. set 1: a toll-free number, set 2: an access number or pin code)? You can use register 02 to add that second string of numbers. You can also add a delay between the first set of numbers (toll-free number) and the second set of numbers using register 06 as follows (10=1 second, 20=2 seconds, 30=3 seconds, 40=4 seconds, 50=5 seconds, etc): ***8888 (hear 1 beep) *01*1*B** (hear 1 beep) *02*1*2*C** *03*1*A1*A2*A3*A4*A5 .... *AN** (hear 1 beep) *06*1*50** *10*1** *15*A1*A2*A3*A4*A5 .... *AN** (hear 1 beep) *17*1*00*3** *18*1234567890** (hear 1 beep) hangup Where A (also A1, A2, A3 ...AN) = the numbers dialed by the users that you want to re-route B = the first set of numbers required by your carrier (access number) C = the second set of numbers required by your carrier (access code) A,B & C are any string of numbers (0-9), up to 16 digits long, including #..
If you need any more help, please contact us.

solution-article.title: How to program to auto dial a number when taken off-hook?
solution-article.description:
Step 1: ***8888 Wait for “BEEP” sound, log in the system.


Step 2: *01*1*number** Wait for “BEEP” sound, set access number.


Step 3: hang up.

Where the number could be any string of digits from 1 to 16 digits long, including #.
solution-article.desc-un-html: Step 1: ***8888 Wait for “BEEP” sound, log in the system. Step 2: *01*1*number** Wait for “BEEP” sound, set access number. Step 3: hang up. Where the number could be any string of digits from 1 to 16 digits long, including #.

solution-article.title: My dialer is dialing but my phone line is not dialing
solution-article.description:
Replace **27** with the following command to change the dialing type:
**278*  (wait for beep)


If problem persists, please contact us.
solution-article.desc-un-html: Replace **27** with the following command to change the dialing type: **278* (wait for beep) If problem persists, please contact us.

solution-article.title: How to program it to auto dial a number and a pin?
solution-article.description:
Below are the instructions:

***  [wait for beep]
**9**  [wait for beep]  
*9*1**200*NUMBER*PIN**  [wait for beep -- programs number and pin to be auto dialed]

*5*1*0050**  [wait for beep -- adds a 5 second delay between number and pin]

**235*  [wait for beep -- enables off-hook dialing]
solution-article.desc-un-html: Below are the instructions: *** [wait for beep] **9** [wait for beep] *9*1**200*NUMBER*PIN** [wait for beep -- programs number and pin to be auto dialed] *5*1*0050** [wait for beep -- adds a 5 second delay between number and pin] **235* [wait for beep -- enables off-hook dialing]

solution-article.title: How to program to auto dial *67 (or * + any number)?
solution-article.description:
Here are the steps:

* * *   (login)
* * 9 * *  (reset)
* 9 * 1 * * 200 * # # 67 * *  (program number to auto dial)
* * 241 *  (changes # by *)
* * 235 *  (enable off-hook dialing)

If you need any more help, please contact us.
solution-article.desc-un-html: Here are the steps: * * * (login) * * 9 * * (reset) * 9 * 1 * * 200 * # # 67 * * (program number to auto dial) * * 241 * (changes # by *) * * 235 * (enable off-hook dialing) If you need any more help, please contact us.

solution-article.title: Can I add a delay between 2 sets of number or digits?
solution-article.description:
Yes, you can! Please follow the instructions below to remote program (remove first line if local programming):

* # 1234 (wait for beep)

* * * (wait for beep)     

* * 9 * * (wait for beep)

* 9 * 1 * * 200 * number1 * number2 * *  (wait for beep -  read note 1)   

* 5 * 1 * 0030 *  (wait for beep -- waits 3 seconds delay between number1 and number2 -- read note 2 below)  
* * 235 * (wait for beep)

* * 27 # * (waif for beep -- allows loud dialing tone, optional)

Hangup

note 1: As "number1", enter the first set of numbers (1-10) that you wish to dial before the pause.  Then, as "number2" enter the second set of numbers (1-10).

note 2: Instead of 0030, you can use 0020 for a 2 second delay, or 0010 for a 1 second delay.
solution-article.desc-un-html: Yes, you can! Please follow the instructions below to remote program (remove first line if local programming): * # 1234 (wait for beep) * * * (wait for beep) * * 9 * * (wait for beep) * 9 * 1 * * 200 * number1 * number2 * * (wait for beep - read note 1) * 5 * 1 * 0030 * (wait for beep -- waits 3 seconds delay between number1 and number2 -- read note 2 below) * * 235 * (wait for beep) * * 27 # * (waif for beep -- allows loud dialing tone, optional) Hangup note 1: As "number1", enter the first set of numbers (1-10) that you wish to dial before the pause. Then, as "number2" enter the second set of numbers (1-10). note 2: Instead of 0030, you can use 0020 for a 2 second delay, or 0010 for a 1 second delay.

solution-article.title: Can I add delays to the dialing numbers (inter-digit delay)?
solution-article.description:
Yes, use the following command lines to add 1 to 2 second inter-digit delays between the dialing number(s):


1 second delay between digits: 

***.    (wait for beep)  
**9** (wait for beep)
*9*1**200*NUMBER** (wait for beep)
**235* (enables off-hook dialing) (wait for beep)
**4*11* (inter-digit timer settings) (wait for beep)

**2110* (wait for beep)
**2210* (wait for beep)


1.5 second delay between digits: 
Replace the last 2 commands for these:

**2115*
**2215*

2 second delay between digits: 
Replace the last 2 commands for these:

**2120*
**2220*


If you are programming it remotely (calling it from another line), then you must use the command below to enter the programming mode: 
*#1234 (wait for beep)
solution-article.desc-un-html: Yes, use the following command lines to add 1 to 2 second inter-digit delays between the dialing number(s): 1 second delay between digits: ***. (wait for beep) **9** (wait for beep) *9*1**200*NUMBER** (wait for beep) **235* (enables off-hook dialing) (wait for beep) **4*11* (inter-digit timer settings) (wait for beep) **2110* (wait for beep) **2210* (wait for beep) 1.5 second delay between digits: Replace the last 2 commands for these: **2115* **2215* 2 second delay between digits: Replace the last 2 commands for these: **2120* **2220* If you are programming it remotely (calling it from another line), then you must use the command below to enter the programming mode: *#1234 (wait for beep)

solution-article.title: Can I block 10-digit numbers?
solution-article.description:
Yes!  We added a new feature (as of 6-12-2021) to allow blocking of up to 3 10-digit numbers on our Programmable Outbound Call Blocker (Product 3232GHQ)
Product details:  https://www.hqtelecom.com/callblockers/outgoingcallblockers/blocker

For example, below are the programming steps to block 305-512-1234 or 1-305-512-1234

***  (hear a beep)

**9**  (hear a beep) 

**5**5121234**  (hear a beep) 

**59*305*1305** (hear a beep)

Tip: If at any time you hear 2 beeps, you need to re-enter that commend until you hear 1 beep.

Similarly, you can block up to 3 10-digit numbers as follows:

Blocking numbers: 305-512-1111, 786-512-2222, and 954-512-3333 (including 1+)

***  (hear a beep)

**9**  (hear a beep) 

**5**5121111*5122222*5123333**  (hear a beep) 

**59*305*1305*786*1786*954*1954** (hear a beep)

Similarly, you can block up to 3 10-digit numbers as follows: Blocking numbers: 305-512-1111, 786-512-2222, and 954-512-3333 (including 1+) *** (hear a beep) **9** (hear a beep) **5**5121111*5122222*5123333** (hear a beep) **59*305*1305*786*1786*954*1954** (hear a beep)

solution-article.title: Can I add a delay prior to the number being auto dialed out?
solution-article.description:
Yes,  please follow these programming steps:

*# 1234                    - entering re-programming mode 
*** (beep)                 - entering instruction set 
** 22 XY * (beep)      - programming register to add delay prior to auto dial number
Hangup


Where 
XY = 10 adds 1 second
XY = 20 adds 2 seconds
XY = 30 adds 3 seconds

etc



For additional product info, please click here

solution-folder.name: Outgoing Phone Blocker w/Allow & Disallow Memory
solution-folder.description: Common issues when programming the Outgoing Phone Blocker w/Allow & Disallow Memory, and useful tips on how to fix them.

solution-article.title: After adding a phone number into memory, when you dial it, the device still allows it to ring once before disconnecting. Is there any way to stop it from ringing the caller at all?
solution-article.description:
This happens sometimes because the user very close to their local Central Office Switch (CO) so the CO picks up the numbers as the user dials them, and completes the calls before the device can cut off (e.g. disconnect) the calls. 


To prevent this, if you are trying to block a 10 digit number, that the calls are still going thru ... keep reducing the number of digits until it works.  For example if you are trying to block calls to 305-558-5577, 
First try blocking 9 digits ... 305-558-557
If it does not work, then then try blocking 8 digits ... 305-558-55
If it does not work, then then try blocking 7 digits ... 305-558-5

If it does not work, then then try blocking 6 digits ... 305-558
6 digits (area code + exchange) should be enough to stop the local CO from dialing the number before the blocker disconnects the call.

This is even more common when blocking to 3 digits e.g. 411 calls.  In those cases, try entering 2 digits into the memory. 

For example if you are trying to block calls to 411, 
First try blocking 2 digits ... 41
If it does not work, then then try blocking 1 digit ... 4

Note: The only issue with this is that it will block all calls that start with 4
solution-article.desc-un-html:
This happens sometimes because the user very close to their local Central Office Switch (CO) so the CO picks up the numbers as the user dials them, and completes the calls before the device can cut off (e.g. disconnect) the calls.
To prevent this, if you are trying to block a 10 digit number, that the calls are still going thru ... keep reducing the number of digits until it works. For example if you are trying to block calls to 305-558-5577, First try blocking 9 digits ... 305-558-557 If it does not work, then then try blocking 8 digits ... 305-558-55 If it does not work, then then try blocking 7 digits ... 305-558-5 If it does not work, then then try blocking 6 digits ... 305-558 6 digits (area code + exchange) should be enough to stop the local CO from dialing the number before the blocker disconnects the call. This is even more common when blocking to 3 digits e.g. 411 calls. In those cases, try entering 2 digits into the memory. For example if you are trying to block calls to 411, First try blocking 2 digits ... 41 If it does not work, then then try blocking 1 digit ... 4 Note: The only issue with this is that it will block all calls that start with 4

folder.name: Outgoing Phone Blocker w/Allow & Disallow Memory
folder.description: Common issues when programming the Outgoing Phone Blocker w/Allow & Disallow Memory, and useful tips on how to fix them.

solution-article.title: Can I disallow all outbound calls except a few?
solution-article.description:
Yes, you can ... To disallow all calls, simply add * to the disallow register 05, as follows:

#0*  

05    

*      

##  
99  

hangup


If you wish to alllow dialing of certain numbers, please use register 08, as follows:

#0* 

05   

*    

##  

08  

(enter number 1 e.g. 305-558-5577)  

##

08 

(enter number 2 e.g. 786-221-5997)

##
(you can keep adding numbers to the allow register)

99
hangup

You should hear a beep after each programming line above.
solution-article.desc-un-html: Yes, you can ... To disallow all calls, simply add * to the disallow register 05, as follows: #0* 05 * ## 99 hangup If you wish to alllow dialing of certain numbers, please use register 08, as follows: #0* 05 * ## 08 (enter number 1 e.g. 305-558-5577) ## 08 (enter number 2 e.g. 786-221-5997) ## (you can keep adding numbers to the allow register) 99 hangup You should hear a beep after each programming line above.

solution-article.title: How can I do a hardware reset?
solution-article.description: Please look at the picture below.  The two pads marked with red arrows need to be shorted when the phone is off hook.  Keep pads shorted until you hear a beep, you are now in program mode and may enter the default programming code 88 ##.
solution-article.desc-un-html: Please look at the picture below. The two pads marked with red arrows need to be shorted when the phone is off hook. Keep pads shorted until you hear a beep, you are now in program mode and may enter the default programming code 88 ##.

solution-article.title: Can I add a delay when auto dialing a number?
solution-article.description:
Yes, you can! Register 12 allows you to add an Off hook dial delay in milliseconds

This register comes into play when using the Hotline dial feature or the speed dial numbers. This register

determines the delay from when the phone goes off hook to when it dials the hotline number. When a

speed dial trigger is detected the dialer has to hang up the line to get dial tone back, the dialer delays

this amount of time after getting dial tone back then dials the speed dial number. Scenario: You want

the dialer to delay 2000 milliseconds (2 seconds) before dialing hotline number. How to:

Step 1) Enter # 0 * -- wait for beep
Step 2) 12 -- wait for beep
Step 3) 2000 -- wait for beep
Step 4) ## -- wait for 2 beeps

Hangup


You can also add delays to the digits as they are being dialed out by programming registers 14 & 15

Register 14 Dtmf ON time in milliseconds
This sets the duration of the dtmf tone of each digit the dialer dials.
Scenario: You want the dtmf tone on time to be 50 milliseconds. How to:

Step 1) Enter # 0 * -- wait for beep

Step 2) 14 -- wait for beep
Step 3) 50 -- wait for beep
Step 4) ## -- waid for 2 beeps

Hangup

Register 15 Dtmf OFF time in milliseconds
This sets the duration of the silence period between each dtmf tone the dialer dials.
Scenario: You want the dtmf tone off time to be 50 milliseconds. How to:

Step 1) Enter # 0 * -- wait for beep

Step 2) 15 -- wait for beep
Step 3) 50 -- wait for beep
Step 4) ## -- waid for 2 beeps

Hangup


Example:  The programing below adds a 2 second delay before start dialing the toll-free number, and also adds a .5 second delay between each digit dialing.

#0*   : Enter program mode
01  18003468080 ##  : Number to dial
12   2000 ##               : Set 2000 millisecond delay before dialing
15   500 ##                 : Set 500 millisecond delay between dialed digits
Hang up


Here another example:
Program your dialer to dial * # 1234, wait 2 seconds, then dial * * *, wait 2 seconds,, then dial * * 2 7 * *
For this case, you can add those delays on the register 01 ...
#0* : enter program code
01 (hear beep)
* #1 1234 #2 #2 #2 #2 * * * #2 #2 #2 #2 * * 27 * * ##
Hang up
Note (1):  Please note that you need to use "#1" for this dialer to dial #

Note (2):  #2 adds a .5 second delay, so if you want a 2 second delay, you need to add it 4 times.

If you need any more help, please contact us.  


>> Buy this hot dialer  | View all dialers
solution-article.desc-un-html: Yes, you can! Register 12 allows you to add an Off hook dial delay in milliseconds This register comes into play when using the Hotline dial feature or the speed dial numbers. This register determines the delay from when the phone goes off hook to when it dials the hotline number. When a speed dial trigger is detected the dialer has to hang up the line to get dial tone back, the dialer delays this amount of time after getting dial tone back then dials the speed dial number. Scenario: You want the dialer to delay 2000 milliseconds (2 seconds) before dialing hotline number. How to: Step 1) Enter # 0 * -- wait for beep Step 2) 12 -- wait for beep Step 3) 2000 -- wait for beep Step 4) ## -- wait for 2 beeps Hangup You can also add delays to the digits as they are being dialed out by programming registers 14 & 15 Register 14 Dtmf ON time in milliseconds This sets the duration of the dtmf tone of each digit the dialer dials. Scenario: You want the dtmf tone on time to be 50 milliseconds. How to: Step 1) Enter # 0 * -- wait for beep Step 2) 14 -- wait for beep Step 3) 50 -- wait for beep Step 4) ## -- waid for 2 beeps Hangup Register 15 Dtmf OFF time in milliseconds This sets the duration of the silence period between each dtmf tone the dialer dials. Scenario: You want the dtmf tone off time to be 50 milliseconds. How to: Step 1) Enter # 0 * -- wait for beep Step 2) 15 -- wait for beep Step 3) 50 -- wait for beep Step 4) ## -- waid for 2 beeps Hangup Example: The programing below adds a 2 second delay before start dialing the toll-free number, and also adds a .5 second delay between each digit dialing. #0* : Enter program mode 01 18003468080 ## : Number to dial 12 2000 ## : Set 2000 millisecond delay before dialing 15 500 ## : Set 500 millisecond delay between dialed digits Hang up Here another example: Program your dialer to dial * # 1234, wait 2 seconds, then dial * * *, wait 2 seconds,, then dial * * 2 7 * * For this case, you can add those delays on the register 01 ... #0* : enter program code 01 (hear beep) * #1 1234 #2 #2 #2 #2 * * * #2 #2 #2 #2 * * 27 * * ## Hang up Note (1): Please note that you need to use "#1" for this dialer to dial # Note (2): #2 adds a .5 second delay, so if you want a 2 second delay, you need to add it 4 times. solution-article.title: Can I stop the sounds of digits being dialed when I pick up the handset?
solution-article.description:
Yes, you can mute the instruct the dialer to mute when it dials so the user can not hear the tones when it dials out via the register 13.

Register 13 Mute dialing
Valid Entries:
0 = No mute 1 = Mute

Scenario: You want the dialer to mute the tones when dialing out. How to:
Step 1) Enter # 0 * -- waif for beep
Step 2) 13 -- wait for beep
Step 3) 1  -- waif for beep
Step 4) ## -- Wait for 2 beeps


If you need any more help, please contact us.  


>> Buy this hot dialer  | View all dialers

Register 13 Mute dialing Valid Entries: 0 = No mute 1 = Mute Scenario: You want the dialer to mute the tones when dialing out. How to: Step 1) Enter # 0 * -- waif for beep Step 2) 13 -- wait for beep Step 3) 1 -- waif for beep Step 4) ## -- Wait for 2 beeps If you need any more help, please contact us. >> Buy this hot dialer | View all dialers

solution-article.title: How do I program this dialer to add a 2 second pause after the auto dial number and then dial another number?
solution-article.description:
Using #2 in register 01 adds .5 seconds.  So, added 4 times to add a delay of 2 seconds, as shown in the example below:

For example, program it to auto dial 3055585577 pause (2 seconds) then dial 1:

Step 1) Enter # 0 *(you should hear a 'Beep' sound) -- entering the programming mode 


Step 2) Enter 01 (you should hear a 'Beep' sound) -- entering function code to restrict numbers 


Step 3) Enter 3055585577 #2#2#2#2 1


Step 4) Hang-up or press ## -- exit
solution-article.desc-un-html: Using #2 in register 01 adds .5 seconds. So, added 4 times to add a delay of 2 seconds, as shown in the example below: For example, program it to auto dial 3055585577 pause (2 seconds) then dial 1: Step 1) Enter # 0 *(you should hear a 'Beep' sound) -- entering the programming mode Step 2) Enter 01 (you should hear a 'Beep' sound) -- entering function code to restrict numbers Step 3) Enter 3055585577 #2#2#2#2 1 Step 4) Hang-up or press ## -- exit

solution-article.title: Can I program it remotely?
solution-article.description:
Yes, you can program this dialer locally or remotely.  If you are experiencing any issues while programming it locally, you should try using a phone line from a different PBX or phone service provider (Tip: consider programming it on a different location).  


You may also be able to program it remotely by calling it from another phone e.g. your cell phone.  Once you answer the call with the handset connected on the dialer, you should be able to input the programming codes as described in the manual.  This works well if there is no compatibility issues among the phone lines from the caller to the receiver.  


For more details on this speed dialer product, please click here.  If you still need help, please call our engineers at 786-221-5997.

If you are experiencing any issues while programming it locally, you should try using a phone line from a different PBX or phone service provider (Tip: consider programming it on a different location). You may also be able to program it remotely by calling it from another phone e.g. your cell phone. Once you answer the call with the handset connected on the dialer, you should be able to input the programming codes as described in the manual. This works well if there is no compatibility issues among the phone lines from the caller to the receiver. For more details on this speed dialer product, please click here. If you still need help, please call our engineers at 786-221-5997.

solution-article.title: How do I know when the number(s) I programmed are being blocked?
solution-article.description:
One way for you to know if the number you programmed was blocked by this device is if you hear a busy signal. 


Another way is if you have a callerID display on the handset you use to dial (or redial) this number and notice that there is a "_I" shaped symbol replacing the first digit of the number being dialed in the display.

Anyways, if you are not sure, you should re-program it. When re-programming it, you should do it in 2 steps ... For example, if you wish to block calls to QVC 1-800-367-9444

Step 1 -- Lift up the handset and dial *** (beep), ** 9 ** (beep) ** 5 ** 3679444 ** (beep) -- hang up

Step 2 --Lift up the handset and dial  *** (beep), ** 59 * 800 * 1800 ** (beep)  -- hang up

If you still have questions, contact us.

Another way is if you have a callerID display on the handset you use to dial (or redial) this number and notice that there is a "_I" shaped symbol replacing the first digit of the number being dialed in the display. Anyways, if you are not sure, you should re-program it. When re-programming it, you should do it in 2 steps ... For example, if you wish to block calls to QVC 1-800-367-9444 Step 1 -- Lift up the handset and dial *** (beep), ** 9 ** (beep) ** 5 ** 3679444 ** (beep) -- hang up Step 2 --Lift up the handset and dial *** (beep), ** 59 * 800 * 1800 ** (beep) -- hang up If you still have questions, contact us.

solution-article.title: Does it require Caller ID service?
solution-article.description: Yes, this device requires the caller id service to be active in order to work properly.

solution-article.title: How does it Work?
solution-article.description: It's very easy to install and use ... there is no programming needed, simply plug it in between the phoneline and your phone (as shown in the manual), and wait for unwanted calls to ring ... then press the BLACKLIST button to add unwanted calls to the BLACKLIST so that next time those numbers call, the unit disconnects those calls.  Optionally, if you have a list of unwanted numbers, you could add unwanted numbers to the BLACKLIST memory manually, but that's not necessary.
solution-article.desc-un-html: It's very easy to install and use ... there is no programming needed, simply plug it in between the phoneline and your phone (as shown in the manual), and wait for unwanted calls to ring ... then press the BLACKLIST button to add unwanted calls to the BLACKLIST so that next time those numbers call, the unit disconnects those calls. Optionally, if you have a list of unwanted numbers, you could add unwanted numbers to the BLACKLIST memory manually, but that's not necessary.

solution-article.description: No, this unit has non-volatile memory that will prevent it from losing all the stored numbers, even when disconnected for a long time.

solution-article.description: You only need one T-Lock to protect your phone line (or phone number), regardless of the how many phone extensions(s) you have, corded or cordless.

solution-article.title: Can I use the T-Lock Call Blocker to block calls to my cell?
solution-article.description: The T-Lock Call Blocker works on landline, not mobiles or cell phone lines.  However, you can forward your cell calls to your landline and use the T-Lock Call Blocker to block the cell calls.
solution-article.desc-un-html: The T-Lock Call Blocker works on landline, not mobiles or cell phone lines. However, you can forward your cell calls to your landline and use the T-Lock Call Blocker to block the cell calls.

solution-article.title: My Call Blocker does not display (or register) the caller ID information on incoming calls
solution-article.description:
Below are some tips and recommendations that may help: 



1) If you have a wi-fi router/modem and/ or a wireless or cordless phone system (e.g. Digital Dec 6.0 or similar) nearby your Call Blocker, we recommend using a longer patch cord in order to allow at least 1-3 feet separation between your Call blocker and your other equipment.  This will help reducing the interference that may be caused by the other telecom equipment which may be affecting the functionality of your call blocker. Please see diagram for illustration.



2) You may also try installing the Call Blocker in parallel using a 2-way splitter (please refer to manual installation option 2),  or using another phone jack (if available) to see if this helps.

3) If you have a VoIP service with a modem e.g. Ooma TELO, you may need to use a special patch cord to reduce the amount of current going into your call blocker.  Please refer to this blog for more details:
https://hqtelecom.com/blog/testing-call-blockers-v5-v6-ooma-telo/.  If you would like to try this special patch cord. please contact us.

4) Also, we have received a few complaints from clients using standard voice services from providers such as AT&T, Comcast, and CenturyLink. However, this issue only affects a very small group of users within each provider, mainly users located in rural areas. We suspect that these providers may be using non-DTMF compatible equipment. Please note that 99.9% of users on AT&T, Comcast, Century Link, etc do not experience this issue.  So, if you still have this issue, then you should contact us to request an exchange.
solution-article.desc-un-html:

1) If you have a wi-fi router/modem and/ or a wireless or cordless phone system (e.g. Digital Dec 6.0 or similar) nearby your Call Blocker, we recommend using a longer patch cord in order to allow at least 1-3 feet separation between your Call blocker and your other equipment. This will help reducing the interference that may be caused by the other telecom equipment which may be affecting the functionality of your call blocker. Please see diagram for illustration. 2) You may also try installing the Call Blocker in parallel using a 2-way splitter (please refer to manual installation option 2), or using another phone jack (if available) to see if this helps.
3) If you have a VoIP service with a modem e.g. Ooma TELO, you may need to use a special patch cord to reduce the amount of current going into your call blocker. Please refer to this blog for more details: https://hqtelecom.com/blog/testing-call-blockers-v5-v6-ooma-telo/. If you would like to try this special patch cord. please contact us.
4) Also, we have received a few complaints from clients using standard voice services from providers such as AT&T, Comcast, and CenturyLink. However, this issue only affects a very small group of users within each provider, mainly users located in rural areas. We suspect that these providers may be using non-DTMF compatible equipment. Please note that 99.9% of users on AT&T, Comcast, Century Link, etc do not experience this issue. So, if you still have this issue, then you should contact us to request an exchange.

folder.name: Call Blocker Model CT-CID803 (Versions 1, 2.0, 2.2, N2 & 5.0)
folder.description: Product Code: CT-CID 803 and CT-CID 803 V5.0

solution-article.title: The YEAR setting (201X) resets often / or Unable to setup the YEAR
solution-article.description:
We recommend that you do not program this parameter.  Below is the reasons why:  

The year setting (e.g. 20XX) is the only parameter that requires manual setting.  However if you change this parameter, please keep in mind that it may reset often (e.g, go back to 2012).  This may happen because this is a line powered device, and it depends on the power available from your phone service (jack or modem).  

The year is not relevant for this device to block unwanted calls.  


The rest of the date/time information (month, day, and time (HH:MM:SS) is automatically updated by the incoming calls' caller id information on these call block devices.  


Please contact us if you have more questions.
solution-article.desc-un-html:
We recommend that you do not program this parameter. Below is the reasons why: The year setting (e.g. 20XX) is the only parameter that requires manual setting. However if you change this parameter, please keep in mind that it may reset often (e.g, go back to 2012). This may happen because this is a line powered device, and it depends on the power available from your phone service (jack or modem). The year is not relevant for this device to block unwanted calls.
The rest of the date/time information (month, day, and time (HH:MM:SS) is automatically updated by the incoming calls' caller id information on these call block devices.
Please contact us if you have more questions.

solution-article.title: I'm unable to program the T-Lock Call Blocker using my Wireless handset
solution-article.description:
We have received this feedback from a few customers using some models of wireless handsets e.g. Vtech model 5153C.  This happens due to a number of reasons:

1) Interference with other telecom equipment
Solution:  Try using a longer cord so you can allow at least 2 feet separation between the Call BLocker and the rest of your Telecom equipment (including modems, wi-fi routers,  phone systems, etc). 

2) Compatibility issues among some wireless handsets
We are investigating this issue.  In the meantime, we recommend that you use a different handset (preferably a cheap corded handset will do!).

IMPORTANT: Please keep in mind that there is no need to pre-program or preset your call blocker when you first get it set up.  This is truly a plug-and-play device that you can setup (in series or parallel), and start blocking unwanted calls by simply pressing the ‘BLACKLIST’ button.
solution-article.desc-un-html: We have received this feedback from a few customers using some models of wireless handsets e.g. Vtech model 5153C. This happens due to a number of reasons: 1) Interference with other telecom equipment Solution: Try using a longer cord so you can allow at least 2 feet separation between the Call BLocker and the rest of your Telecom equipment (including modems, wi-fi routers, phone systems, etc). 2) Compatibility issues among some wireless handsets We are investigating this issue. In the meantime, we recommend that you use a different handset (preferably a cheap corded handset will do!). IMPORTANT: Please keep in mind that there is no need to pre-program or preset your call blocker when you first get it set up. This is truly a plug-and-play device that you can setup (in series or parallel), and start blocking unwanted calls by simply pressing the ‘BLACKLIST’ button.

solution-article.title: Can I block calls that have no Caller ID info, and are shown as ‘unidentified’, or ‘unknown’, or ‘private’, or ‘Out of Area’, etc?
solution-article.description: Yes. The T-LOCK has a new feature (refer to section 6 of the manual). This feature is turned “OFF” by default, however if you turn it “ON”, it will block all incoming calls with no caller ID info.
solution-article.desc-un-html: Yes. The T-LOCK has a new feature (refer to section 6 of the manual). This feature is turned “OFF” by default, however if you turn it “ON”, it will block all incoming calls with no caller ID info.

folder.description: Product Code: CT-CID 803 and CT-CID 803 V5.0

solution-article.title: Clarification about the quantity of numbers that can be stored in the T-Lock Call Blocker
solution-article.description:
1,500 is the # of phone & fax numbers you can store in the Blacklist memory.  


The 100 is the # of incoming calls that the unit stores in the display memory. 


The 50 is the # of outbound calls the unit stores in the display memory.


All the above numbers will be stored permanently in non-volatile memory.  This means that your blocked numbers till remained in memory even if you loose power.
solution-article.desc-un-html:
1,500 is the # of phone & fax numbers you can store in the Blacklist memory.
The 100 is the # of incoming calls that the unit stores in the display memory.
The 50 is the # of outbound calls the unit stores in the display memory.
All the above numbers will be stored permanently in non-volatile memory. This means that your blocked numbers till remained in memory even if you loose power.

folder.name: Call Blocker Model CT-CID803 (Versions 1, 2.0, 2.2, N2 & 5.0)
folder.description: Product Code: CT-CID 803 and CT-CID 803 V5.0

solution-article.title: Can I manually add phone numbers to the Blacklist?
solution-article.description:
Yes, you can manually add a list of telephone numbers to the Blacklist memory of your Blocker. 

There are 3 ways of adding numbers to the BLACKLIST.
1) Wait for the unwanted call to ring, and press the "BLACKLIST" button (Recommended)
2) Follow step 5.0 in the manual at http://hqtelecom.com/TLock-Pro-CallBlocker-N2-UserManual.pdf
3) To add these numbers to the blacklist, follow these steps:

a) Connect a telephone to the PHONE port for your Blocker.

b) Dial a number you want to block using the dial-pad of the telephone connected to the PHONE port (you can hang up).

c) Now, press OUT button to view the outbound calls.  Then use the scrolling Left/Right buttons to find the number you just dialed.  Press the “Blacklist” button to add it to the backlist memory. Repeat these steps for each number you want to block.
solution-article.desc-un-html: Yes, you can manually add a list of telephone numbers to the Blacklist memory of your Blocker. There are 3 ways of adding numbers to the BLACKLIST. 1) Wait for the unwanted call to ring, and press the "BLACKLIST" button (Recommended) 2) Follow step 5.0 in the manual at http://hqtelecom.com/TLock-Pro-CallBlocker-N2-UserManual.pdf 3) To add these numbers to the blacklist, follow these steps: a) Connect a telephone to the PHONE port for your Blocker. b) Dial a number you want to block using the dial-pad of the telephone connected to the PHONE port (you can hang up). c) Now, press OUT button to view the outbound calls. Then use the scrolling Left/Right buttons to find the number you just dialed. Press the “Blacklist” button to add it to the backlist memory. Repeat these steps for each number you want to block.

folder.name: Call Blocker Model CT-CID803 (Versions 1, 2.0, 2.2, N2 & 5.0)
folder.description: Product Code: CT-CID 803 and CT-CID 803 V5.0

solution-article.title: T-Lock Installation Options (Series and versus Parallel - Pros and Cons)
solution-article.description:
There are 2 ways to install your blocker (please refer to manual). There are Pros and Cons of each setup, as follows:

Series Installation:


Pros:

- You may not hear any rings when a blacklisted number calls you.
- You don’t need to change your setup to program your T-Lock.


Cons:

- You may lose the Caller ID info on your existing phones. This happens with many phone services due to the fact that this unit is line powered and there is not enough signal strength on your phone line to pass the caller ID info to the phone(s).

Parallel Installation:


Pros:

- You will be able to continue receiving the caller ID information on your existing phones.
- If you have low power on your phone line, this setup will work better for your existing phones.


Cons:

- You still need to connect a handset to the ‘PHONE’ socket in order to program your T-Lock.
- Also, you will hear 1-2 more rings when blacklisted numbers call you.

To learn more about series and parallel, please read the manual.
solution-article.desc-un-html:
There are 2 ways to install your blocker (please refer to manual). There are Pros and Cons of each setup, as follows:
Series Installation:
Pros:
- You may not hear any rings when a blacklisted number calls you. - You don’t need to change your setup to program your T-Lock.
Cons:
- You may lose the Caller ID info on your existing phones. This happens with many phone services due to the fact that this unit is line powered and there is not enough signal strength on your phone line to pass the caller ID info to the phone(s).
Parallel Installation:
Pros:
- You will be able to continue receiving the caller ID information on your existing phones. - If you have low power on your phone line, this setup will work better for your existing phones.
Cons:
- You still need to connect a handset to the ‘PHONE’ socket in order to program your T-Lock. - Also, you will hear 1-2 more rings when blacklisted numbers call you.
To learn more about series and parallel, please read the manual.

folder.name: Call Blocker Model CT-CID803 (Versions 1, 2.0, 2.2, N2 & 5.0)
folder.description: Product Code: CT-CID 803 and CT-CID 803 V5.0

solution-article.title: How many rings do I hear on blocked calls? Will my phone still ring on incoming calls from numbers that are in the BLACKLIST?
solution-article.description:
The number of rings depends on 2 factors:

1) If you connect your phone(s) in series (as shown above), your phone(s) should not ring on blacklisted numbers. If you connect it in parallel, your phone(s) may ring 1-2 times before the call blocker disconnect the blacklisted calls.


2) Also, most telephone providers send the Caller id information ahead of the ring signals, giving the call blocker enough time to check the blacklist and ‘suppress’ the first ring signals. However, there are a few telephone providers that send the caller id info at the same time they send the ring signal, allowing no time for the call blocker to suppress the rings. In these cases, you may ring 1-2 rings, even if you have it connected in series.
solution-article.desc-un-html: The number of rings depends on 2 factors: 1) If you connect your phone(s) in series (as shown above), your phone(s) should not ring on blacklisted numbers. If you connect it in parallel, your phone(s) may ring 1-2 times before the call blocker disconnect the blacklisted calls. 2) Also, most telephone providers send the Caller id information ahead of the ring signals, giving the call blocker enough time to check the blacklist and ‘suppress’ the first ring signals. However, there are a few telephone providers that send the caller id info at the same time they send the ring signal, allowing no time for the call blocker to suppress the rings. In these cases, you may ring 1-2 rings, even if you have it connected in series.

solution-article.title: I’m getting lots of random digits in the incoming list
solution-article.description:
Based on what we have been able to troubleshoot, this is due to noise on the line generated by the local phone services.  We have seen this issue in some VOIP services.  Below are some suggestions on how to work around this issue:   The following are possible fixes that have helped other clients reduce the amount of random digits and display freezes:

1) Install a ‘DSL filter” between the Vonage modem and your Call Blocker, using the ‘Voice” jack of the modem to connect to the Blocker.

2) Other clients have been able to regain control of the unit by simply unplug the blocker for 1-2 minutes.

3) Other clients wait for an incoming call to re-gain control of the unit.

4) Other clients request an exchange for our new Landline Call Blocker model which has its own power supply and batteries -- (We will apply full credit for the price you paid, just pay the difference between what you paid for your call blocker and this model, and return the original unit to us).

Related Article: http://help.hqtelecom.com/solution/articles/5000004525-my-display-is-getting-freeze-frozen-e-g-none-of-the-buttons-work-and-or-getting-random-digits-
solution-article.desc-un-html: Based on what we have been able to troubleshoot, this is due to noise on the line generated by the local phone services. We have seen this issue in some VOIP services. Below are some suggestions on how to work around this issue: The following are possible fixes that have helped other clients reduce the amount of random digits and display freezes: 1) Install a ‘DSL filter” between the Vonage modem and your Call Blocker, using the ‘Voice” jack of the modem to connect to the Blocker. 2) Other clients have been able to regain control of the unit by simply unplug the blocker for 1-2 minutes. 3) Other clients wait for an incoming call to re-gain control of the unit. 4) Other clients request an exchange for our new Landline Call Blocker model which has its own power supply and batteries -- (We will apply full credit for the price you paid, just pay the difference between what you paid for your call blocker and this model, and return the original unit to us). Related Article: http://help.hqtelecom.com/solution/articles/5000004525-my-display-is-getting-freeze-frozen-e-g-none-of-the-buttons-work-and-or-getting-random-digits-

folder.description: Product Code: CT-CID 803 and CT-CID 803 V5.0

solution-article.title: My display is getting freeze (frozen) (e.g. none of the buttons work!) and/or getting random digits
solution-article.description:
Based on what we have been able to troubleshoot, this is due to excessive noise on the line generated by the local phone services.  We have seen this issue in some VOIP services. 

Below are some suggestions to fix it and/or work around this issue:


1) Contact your phone service provider and ask them to tune the power / voltage signal levels coming into your house's line to make sure they are normal.

2) Clients have been able to regain control of the unit by simply unplugging their blocker for a minute or so from the LINE cable.


3) Another way to reduce the noise is by using a line filter e.g. DSL filter (installing it in the LINE port of the call blocker), which has helped some customers reduce these ‘freeze’ conditions.  Buy it on eBay > DSL Phone Line Filter 


4) Other clients wait for an incoming call to re-gain control of the unit.

5) Other clients have been able to eliminate the "freezing" effect by simply switching the patch cable that connects the LINE on the call blocker. 

6) Other clients request an exchange for our new Landline Call Blocker model (https://hqtelecom.com/callblacklist)

which has its own power supply and batteries -- (We will apply full credit for the price you paid, just pay the difference between what you paid for your call blocker and this model, and return the original unit to us).

If you still have questions, please contact us.

Below are some suggestions to fix it and/or work around this issue: 1) Contact your phone service provider and ask them to tune the power / voltage signal levels coming into your house's line to make sure they are normal. 2) Clients have been able to regain control of the unit by simply unplugging their blocker for a minute or so from the LINE cable.
3) Another way to reduce the noise is by using a line filter e.g. DSL filter (installing it in the LINE port of the call blocker), which has helped some customers reduce these ‘freeze’ conditions. Buy it on eBay > DSL Phone Line Filter 4) Other clients wait for an incoming call to re-gain control of the unit. 5) Other clients have been able to eliminate the "freezing" effect by simply switching the patch cable that connects the LINE on the call blocker. 6) Other clients request an exchange for our new Landline Call Blocker model (https://hqtelecom.com/callblacklist) which has its own power supply and batteries -- (We will apply full credit for the price you paid, just pay the difference between what you paid for your call blocker and this model, and return the original unit to us). If you still have questions, please contact us.

folder.name: Call Blocker Model CT-CID803 (Versions 1, 2.0, 2.2, N2 & 5.0)

solution-article.title: My call blocker seem to have a protective film covering the LCD display. Should I remove it? How?
solution-article.description:
Yes.  We would recommend that you remove this plastic firm using a thin plastic card (preferably thinner than a credit card).  Carefully insert a corner of the plastic card on the top edge of the LCD display, and then try scraping off the plastic firm slowly.  Once you manage to scrape off a large enough piece of firm to get a grip of it with your hand, gently peel off the rest of the plastic firm from the LCD display.
Watch this video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=udLi0X9oBr8

Carefully insert a corner of the plastic card on the top edge of the LCD display, and then try scraping off the plastic firm slowly. Once you manage to scrape off a large enough piece of firm to get a grip of it with your hand, gently peel off the rest of the plastic firm from the LCD display. Watch this video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=udLi0X9oBr8

solution-article.title: I have call waiting. Can the T-Lock Call Blocker block calls while I'm on another call?
solution-article.description: Unfortunately No.  The T-Lock Call Blocker can only block calls IF you are not on another phoneline.  To the best of our knowledge, there is no call blocker device in the market that can do this!

solution-article.title: Do I need to purchase a second T-Lock Call Blocker if I have 2 phone lines and a 2-line phone?
solution-article.description: Yes, you will need a T-Lock Call Blocker per phone line or phone number.
solution-article.desc-un-html: Yes, you will need a T-Lock Call Blocker per phone line or phone number.

solution-article.title: Does the T-Lock support Name Display?
solution-article.description: Unfortunately no, this device only support number display as part of the caller ID information. 2 reasons:  (1) This product was designed to work in conjunction with your existing phone systems which in its majority support name display. (2) Adding the Bellcore Caller ID chipset will make this product a lot more expensive!

(2) Adding the Bellcore Caller ID chipset will make this product a lot more expensive!

solution-article.title: What does the blocked caller hear when they call?
solution-article.description: The blocked caller only hears one ring tone in his/her phone handset and then silence. After about 10 seconds, then he/she will get a dial-tone or busy signal depending on your phone service provider. The T-Lock Call Blocker produces no sounds or recordings.
solution-article.desc-un-html: The blocked caller only hears one ring tone in his/her phone handset and then silence. After about 10 seconds, then he/she will get a dial-tone or busy signal depending on your phone service provider. The T-Lock Call Blocker produces no sounds or recordings.

solution-article.title: My device cannot block outgoing numbers?
solution-article.description:
Here are some tips:
1) If you are on a 10-digit local dialing, try entering the leading 1 + number.
2) Try entering the first 9 digits of the 10-digit number.

3) Try entering the first 8 digits of the 10-digit number.
Note: This happens because some central office switches handle outbound calls differently so the call blocker is not able to block the dialing out of numbers after certain numbers have been dialed out. .
solution-article.desc-un-html: Here are some tips: 1) If you are on a 10-digit local dialing, try entering the leading 1 + number. 2) Try entering the first 9 digits of the 10-digit number. 3) Try entering the first 8 digits of the 10-digit number. Note: This happens because some central office switches handle outbound calls differently so the call blocker is not able to block the dialing out of numbers after certain numbers have been dialed out. .

solution-article.title: My Call Blocker is hanging up on everyone. What can I do?
solution-article.description:
Below are some troubleshooting tips:

(1) This may be happening if you added by mistake a prefix or area code or 1 or 2 digits to the BLACKLIST.  To fix this, you will need to remove this (these) number(s) from the BLACKLIST.  To do so, please press the BLACKLIST button and the use the scroll buttons to browse the list of phone numbers you have added to the BLACKLIST.  If you find a single digit or 2 digit number there, press the REMOVE button to delete from the BLACKLIST.

(2) If problem continues, clear all the numbers in the BLACKLIST by pressing BLACKLIST, and then holding the REMOVE button for more than 3 seconds.  You will be prompted to enter the PASSword, which is 0000 (default).  NOTE: You will need to have a handset connected on the PHONE port of the call blocker in order to enter the password.

(3) Another possible solution may consist on you having your call blocker installed in parallel using a 2-way splitter.  Please refer to the manual for more details.

(4) Another possible reason why this is happening is due to interference.  Please make sure your call blocker is at least 2 feet away from any other telecom equipment (phones, modems, etc).


If problem continues, please contact our technical support team.  You may need to send it in for repair or replacement under the warranty.
solution-article.desc-un-html: Below are some troubleshooting tips: (1) This may be happening if you added by mistake a prefix or area code or 1 or 2 digits to the BLACKLIST. To fix this, you will need to remove this (these) number(s) from the BLACKLIST. To do so, please press the BLACKLIST button and the use the scroll buttons to browse the list of phone numbers you have added to the BLACKLIST. If you find a single digit or 2 digit number there, press the REMOVE button to delete from the BLACKLIST. (2) If problem continues, clear all the numbers in the BLACKLIST by pressing BLACKLIST, and then holding the REMOVE button for more than 3 seconds. You will be prompted to enter the PASSword, which is 0000 (default). NOTE: You will need to have a handset connected on the PHONE port of the call blocker in order to enter the password. (3) Another possible solution may consist on you having your call blocker installed in parallel using a 2-way splitter. Please refer to the manual for more details. (4) Another possible reason why this is happening is due to interference. Please make sure your call blocker is at least 2 feet away from any other telecom equipment (phones, modems, etc). If problem continues, please contact our technical support team. You may need to send it in for repair or replacement under the warranty.

folder.name: Call Blocker Model CT-CID803 (Versions 1, 2.0, 2.2, N2 & 5.0)
folder.description: Product Code: CT-CID 803 and CT-CID 803 V5.0

solution-article.title: After installing the call blocker, my phone is no longer ringing (my call blocker is blocking all incoming calls). Please advice.
solution-article.description:
Below are some reasons that may cause the call blocker to block all incoming calls:

1) There is a prefix or area code in the BLACKLIST that may cause this.  Please make sure you remove all numbers less than 3 digits from single the BLACKLIST.

2) The feature to block calls with no caller ID is ON.  Please make sure you turn OFF this feature (SET 6) following step 6 in the manual.


3) If you still have this issue, try installing the call blocker in parallel using a 2-way splitter or another phone jack (if available).

4) If none of the above works, please contact us.
solution-article.desc-un-html:
Below are some reasons that may cause the call blocker to block all incoming calls: 1) There is a prefix or area code in the BLACKLIST that may cause this. Please make sure you remove all numbers less than 3 digits from single the BLACKLIST. 2) The feature to block calls with no caller ID is ON. Please make sure you turn OFF this feature (SET 6) following step 6 in the manual.
3) If you still have this issue, try installing the call blocker in parallel using a 2-way splitter or another phone jack (if available). 4) If none of the above works, please contact us.

solution-article.title: How to determine if your call blocker is defective?
solution-article.description: One easy way to determinate if your call blocker is defective is by testing it using a different phone line.  If you do not have a second line at home, you may take it to your neighbors or relative and plug it in to confirm that it’s acting the same way.  If it continues to act the same way, then most likely you have a defective device. If it does not, then the device is okay, and most likely there is a compatibility issue with your phone line.
solution-article.desc-un-html: One easy way to determinate if your call blocker is defective is by testing it using a different phone line. If you do not have a second line at home, you may take it to your neighbors or relative and plug it in to confirm that it’s acting the same way. If it continues to act the same way, then most likely you have a defective device. If it does not, then the device is okay, and most likely there is a compatibility issue with your phone line.

folder.name: Call Blocker Model CT-CID803 (Versions 1, 2.0, 2.2, N2 & 5.0)
folder.description: Product Code: CT-CID 803 and CT-CID 803 V5.0

solution-article.title: My Call Blocker is blocking calls that are not in the BLACKLIST
solution-article.description:
If your call blocker is blocking numbers that you have not added to the BLACKLIST, we recommend that you review your BLACKLIST to make sure there is no single or double digits stored that may be causing the device to block calls you do not want to block.  You can review the BLACKLIST, simply press the BLACKLIST button and then the scroll buttons to scroll up/down the list.  If you see a number that you wish to remove from the BLACKLIST, press the REMOVE button once (Warning:  If you press and hold the REMOVE button for more than 3 seconds it will prompt for the PASSword so that you can delete the entire list).

If the issue persists, you may need to clear the BLACKLIST.  To do this, press the BLACKLIST button, then hold the REMOVE button more than 3 seconds.  It will display PASS, then lift-off a handset (make sure you have one connected to the PHONE port of the call blocker), and press 0000 using the dial-pad, then press the "SET" button on the call blocker.  It should display "SUCCESS".

Another reason that may be causing your call blocker to block numbers that are not in the BLACKLIST, is if you added any prefixes or area codes using the SET function 5 in the manual, refer to step 5 on the manual (http://hqtelecom.com/TLock-Pro-CallBlocker-N2-UserManual.pdf).  

Once you have cleared the BLACKLIST, it should stop blocking those local calls.

If you continue having issues, you may need to send it for repair and/or replacement, if it's under the warranty.

If you see a number that you wish to remove from the BLACKLIST, press the REMOVE button once (Warning: If you press and hold the REMOVE button for more than 3 seconds it will prompt for the PASSword so that you can delete the entire list). If the issue persists, you may need to clear the BLACKLIST. To do this, press the BLACKLIST button, then hold the REMOVE button more than 3 seconds. It will display PASS, then lift-off a handset (make sure you have one connected to the PHONE port of the call blocker), and press 0000 using the dial-pad, then press the "SET" button on the call blocker. It should display "SUCCESS".
